body.label: thm:A2-equals-minus-I-implies-n-even

hypothesis.statement: Let A be an n √ó n matrix with entries in the field of real numbers ‚Ñù such that A^2 = -I_n, where I_n is the n √ó n identity matrix.

body.claim: If A^2 = -I_n for an n √ó n real matrix A, then n is an even integer.

proof.assumption: A is an n √ó n real matrix satisfying A^2 = -I_n.

proof.claim: Viewing A as a linear operator on the real vector space ‚Ñù^n, the equation A^2 = -I_n implies that A satisfies the polynomial equation A^2 + I_n = 0, so the minimal polynomial m_A(x) of A divides p(x) := x^2 + 1.
proof.proof_method: Substitute A into the polynomial x^2 + 1 and use the definition of minimal polynomial.

proof.claim: The polynomial x^2 + 1 has distinct complex roots i and ‚àíi, so it has no repeated roots, and consequently any divisor of x^2 + 1, in particular the minimal polynomial m_A(x), also has no repeated roots.
proof.proof_method: Compute the roots of x^2 + 1 in ‚ÑÇ and use that divisors of a separable polynomial are separable.

proof.claim: Since A is a real matrix whose minimal polynomial has no repeated roots, A is diagonalizable over ‚ÑÇ; thus there exists a basis of ‚ÑÇ^n such that the matrix of A is diagonal, and all eigenvalues of A (over ‚ÑÇ) are roots of x^2 + 1, hence each eigenvalue is either i or ‚àíi.
proof.proof_method: Use the standard result that a linear operator whose minimal polynomial has no repeated roots is diagonalizable over an algebraically closed field.

results_used.statement: A linear operator over a field whose minimal polynomial has no repeated roots is diagonalizable over any field over which the minimal polynomial splits.

proof.properties: eigenvalues of A over ‚ÑÇ listed with algebraic multiplicity, each in {i, ‚àíi}
proof.statement: Let Œª_1, ‚Ä¶, Œª_n denote the eigenvalues of A over ‚ÑÇ, listed with algebraic multiplicity; then each Œª_j is either i or ‚àíi.
Let k be the number of indices j such that Œª_j = i; then the number of indices j such that Œª_j = ‚àíi is n ‚àí k.

proof.claim: The determinant of A, viewed as a complex matrix, satisfies det(A) = ‚àè_{j=1}^n Œª_j = i^k ¬∑ (‚àíi)^{n‚àík}.
proof.proof_method: For a diagonalizable matrix, the determinant equals the product of its eigenvalues counted with algebraic multiplicity.

proof.claim: From the matrix identity A^2 = ‚àíI_n, taking determinants on both sides and using multiplicativity of the determinant gives det(A^2) = det(‚àíI_n), so (det A)^2 = (‚àí1)^n.
proof.proof_method: Apply det to both sides of A^2 = ‚àíI_n, use det(A^2) = (det A)^2 and det(‚àíI_n) = (‚àí1)^n det(I_n) = (‚àí1)^n.

proof.claim: Since A has real entries, det A is a real number, so (det A)^2 is a nonnegative real number; therefore (‚àí1)^n must be a nonnegative real number, forcing (‚àí1)^n = 1.
proof.proof_method: Use that the square of a real number is ‚â• 0 and that (‚àí1)^n ‚àà {1, ‚àí1}.

proof.claim: The equality (‚àí1)^n = 1 holds if and only if n is an even integer.
proof.proof_method: Use the basic parity property that (‚àí1)^n = 1 exactly when n is even.

proof.claim: Therefore n is an even integer.
